main_claims.claim: Increasing scalar-field mass strongly suppresses damping for several branches, signaling an approach to quasi-resonant, very long-lived oscillations.
main_claims.notes: Supported by the stated agreement between WKB-Padé and time-domain integration, which show decreasing imaginary parts of the frequencies.

main_claims.claim: WKB and time-domain evolution show sub-percent relative difference in the parameter space where the potential peak is well defined.
main_claims.notes: The author provides quantitative discrepancy values (e.g., 0.16% and 0.45% relative differences) justifying the reliability of the methods.

main_claims.claim: The dilaton-induced shifts in the quasinormal spectra are substantially larger than the estimated numerical uncertainty.
main_claims.notes: The stated shift is ~35%, while WKB mismatch/error is reported at ~0.5%.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The effective potential maintains a standard barrier shape with a single dominant maximum across the entirety of the sampled parameter space prior to the quasi-resonance threshold.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: If the potential develops multiple extrema or loses its local maximum prematurely as the field mass increases, the 6th-16th order WKB formula becomes entirely invalid and can produce spurious roots. The author notes this possibility but does not rigorously define the phase space boundary where WKB was safely truncated.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Are the data tables (I-V) and equations (1-18) accurately populated in the actual PDF?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The provided HTML-to-text extraction has stripped the contents of all equations and data tables, rendering them as blank structures (e.g., `| | | | | |`), preventing numerical verification of the results.

novelty_assessment: The methodology (WKB-Padé + time-domain integration) and the phenomenology (massive scalar fields yielding quasi-resonances) are both well-established in the literature. The primary novelty lies in the specific application of these techniques to map out the parameter space of the Einstein-Maxwell-dilaton black hole. It is a solid, incremental contribution to the catalog of black hole ringdown signatures in extended gravities.
score_rationale: The paper presents a standard, methodologically sound analysis using established tools (WKB and time-domain integration) that cross-validate each other well. The technical soundness is good, limited only by the acknowledged breakdown of WKB near the real axis. The novelty and significance are moderate ('routine'), as it maps known phenomena (quasi-resonances of massive fields) to a specific parameterized black hole background (EMD). The overall score reflects a competent, incremental phenomenological study.
summary: This paper investigates the quasinormal modes (QNMs) of massive scalar perturbations in the background of static, spherically symmetric Einstein-Maxwell-dilaton (EMD) black holes. The author employs higher-order WKB-Padé approximations alongside a time-domain integration scheme (Gundlach-Price-Pullin) to compute the complex frequencies. The main finding is that increasing the mass of the scalar field strongly suppresses the damping rate, leading to quasi-resonant, long-lived oscillation modes. The paper argues that these shifts and the onset of quasi-resonances are robust physical signatures dependent on the dilaton coupling parameter, and are not numerical artifacts.

technical_objections.evidence: WKB inherently breaks down when the imaginary part of the frequency approaches zero (as the classical turning points of the effective potential merge or behave pathologically). The author acknowledges this limitation but still relies on polynomial/spline extrapolations of the WKB data (Fig. 6) to formalize the zero-crossing.
technical_objections.objection: Reliance on extrapolated WKB results near the real-frequency axis to declare an 'unambiguous signal' of quasi-resonances is overly confident without independent confirmation via a convergent frequency-domain method.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section IV (discussion of extrapolations in Fig. 6) and Section V
